mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the structure and function of animal nervous systems, covering neurons and synaptic transmission via neurotransmitters (D.2.c.i, D.2.c.ii), the central nervous system comprising brain and spinal cord (D.4.a), the peripheral nervous system (D.4.b), and autonomic regulation of heartbeat, digestion, and breathing.

Directly examines the biological clock and circadian periodicity, covering solar-day rhythms across bacteria, plants, fungi, and animals (E.2), the internal generation mechanisms of circadian clocks (E.3), and how external stimuli such as light and darkness modulate these periodicities (E.4).

Directly covers endocrine systems and hormonal coordination (C), including the relationship between endocrine and neural regulation (C.1), the structure and function of vertebrate endocrine glands producing over eighty hormones (C.2.b), and the role of feedback loops in maintaining homeostasis of metabolism, growth, and stress response (A.1, A.2).

Addresses sensory reception and processing across multiple modalities including vision, touch, smell, taste, and hearing corresponding to photoreceptors, mechanoreceptors, and chemoreceptors (B.1.b), cross-modal sensory interactions between adjacent sense cells (B.3), and developmental aspects of specialized sensory processing structures (B.2).

Examines chemical signaling via pheromones functioning through chemoreceptors (B.1.b) and operating as endocrine-like regulatory mechanisms in invertebrates including insects where pheromones govern caste development (C.3), illustrating the relationship between chemical and neural regulation (C.1).

Covers nociceptive sensory reception through mechanoreceptors (B.1.b), interactions between sensory neurons in pain signal processing (B.3), nerve impulse transmission along neural pathways (D.2.c.ii), and brain-level processing of pain signals within the central nervous system (D.4.a).

Examines vertebrate brain structure including neural processes underlying memory and cognition (D.4.a), and the biodynamics of the nervous system as it relates to consciousness and subjective experience (D.4.e), with discussion of neurons, synapses, and the implications of mechanistic brain function.

Addresses brain structures including the hippocampus and amygdala within the central nervous system that support memory encoding, storage, and retrieval (D.4.a), and the biodynamics of neural processes underlying short-term, long-term, episodic, semantic, and procedural memory systems (D.4.e).

Surveys nervous system complexity at the cellular level, detailing neurons, axons, and synapses within brain tissue (D.2.c.i, D.2.c.ii) and the search for the neural basis of consciousness within the central nervous system (D.4.a), illustrating the staggering scale of neural coordination.

Covers embryonic and early development of the vertebrate nervous system including critical periods for synapse formation and pruning (D.4.c), brain structures supporting innate cognitive capacities (D.4.a), and the biodynamics of neural development from number sense and object permanence to theory of mind (D.4.e).
